ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Post Honey Bunches of Oats brand cereal. FOODON_03301710 (breakfast cereal food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E (Breakfast cereals) is the closest defensible category. No brand-specific FoodEx2 code exists for Post Honey Bunches of Oats.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 30g dry cereal (approximately 1.5 cups), typically consumed with milk; a realistic bowl portion in Singapore is often 50–60g.

nutrient_source.title: Post Honey Bunches of Oats Honey Roasted — Nutrition Information Panel (Singapore import label) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central: Cereals ready-to-eat, POST, HONEY BUNCHES OF OATS, honey roasted (FDC ID 173282)
nutrient_source.organization: Post Consumer Brands USA / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173282 (Post Honey Bunches of Oats Honey Roasted), an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values are consistent with the Singapore import NIP label provided in the input record. USDA Branded Food entry is used as the primary approved-source anchor.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of honey-sweetened oat-corn flake breakfast cereal — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for sweetened oat-corn flake cereal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~71 (high) is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for honey-sweetened oat-corn flake breakfast cereals. The University of Sydney GI Database lists sweetened ready-to-eat oat-corn flake cereals in the GI range 67–74. A value of 71 is used as the central estimate, consistent with the honey-sugar coating raising the effective GI above that of plain oat cereals (GI ~55). Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (71 × 24.6) / 100 ≈ 17.5.

hypertension.guidance: At 135mg sodium per 30g dry serving, a single bowl contributes approximately 6–9% of the 1500–2300mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive patients; sodium intake remains manageable if portion size is controlled and no additional salt or high-sodium milk is added, but larger realistic portions (50–60g) approach 225–270mg sodium per bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~71), high added sugar (25g/100g), and limited effective beta-glucan benefit due to sugar coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of ~71 (equivalent to white bread) and 7.5g of sugar per 30g serving (15g per realistic 60g bowl), Post Honey Bunches of Oats Honey Roasted is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the honey-sugar coating negates the glucose-moderating benefit of the oat base, and the product should be replaced with low-GI, high-fibre alternatives such as plain rolled oats or bran-based cereals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (25g/100g) and ultra-processed formulation; low saturated fat (1.5g/100g) and zero trans fat are positive factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While saturated fat (1.5g/100g) and trans fat (0g/100g) are within acceptable limits for cardiovascular disease management, the high added sugar content (25g/100g) contributes to postprandial triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance, both of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors; patients with CVD should limit portion size to 30g and prefer unsweetened whole grain cereals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content and elevated GI promote postprandial insulin spikes and pro-inflammatory cytokine release
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high sugar content (25g/100g) and GI of ~71 may contribute to postprandial inflammatory signalling; patients with rheumatoid arthritis are advised to minimise high-GI, high-sugar breakfast cereals and choose anti-inflammatory alternatives such as plain oats with berries.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Post Honey Bunches of Oats Honey Roasted is a ready-to-eat breakfast cereal manufactured by Post Consumer Brands (USA) and imported into Singapore, where it is sold at Cold Storage, FairPrice Finest, and Giant supermarkets. The cereal consists of a blend of light, crispy corn-and-oat flakes combined with honey-sweetened oat clusters, giving it a varied texture of crunchy flakes and denser, slightly chewy clusters. The flavour profile is sweet and mildly honeyed, with a toasted grain background. It is typically consumed dry or with cold milk. In Singapore, the product is popular with health-conscious adults and expatriate families who associate the 'whole grain oats' and 'honey' branding with nutritional benefit — a classic health-halo effect. Clinically, the honey-sugar coating raises the effective glycemic index to approximately 71 (high GI, equivalent to white bread), and the total sugar content is 25g per 100g (7.5g per standard 30g serving; approximately 15g per realistic 60g bowl). This makes the product nutritionally comparable to Kellogg's Corn Flakes for blood glucose management purposes. The oat content is genuine but its beta-glucan benefit is overwhelmed by the sugar coating. Per 100g: 400 kcal, 8g protein, 6.7g fat, 82g carbohydrate, 25g sugar, 5g dietary fibre, 450mg sodium. The product is fortified with B vitamins (thiamin, riboflavin, niacin, B6, B12, folate) and iron. Not recommended for Type 2 Diabetes patients without strict portion control. Moderate sodium concern for hypertension at larger serving sizes.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173282 (Post Honey Bunches of Oats Honey Roasted), an exact branded product match in an approved secondary global source, consistent with the Singapore import NIP label. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for honey-sweetened oat-corn flake cereals, with confidence 0.9 as stated in the input record.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) is not reported on the Singapore import NIP label and is not available in the USDA FoodData Central branded entry for this product (FDC ID 173282) as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1.0: moisture content for this ready-to-eat dry cereal is not reported in the Singapore NIP or USDA branded entry. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per Singapore import NIP label and USDA FoodData Central entry — product formulation contains no partially hydrogenated oils. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per NIP label — product contains no animal-derived fat ingredients. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg are 0.0: confirmed zero or not fortified per Singapore import NIP label. 6. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for honey-sweetened oat-corn flake cereals; direct GI testing data for this specific Post product variant was not located in the University of Sydney database as of April 2026. 7. Halal status: not confirmed on Singapore import label; marked false pending verification. 8. Allergen note: product may contain traces of wheat and soy depending on manufacturing facility; oats are a primary ingredient.
